ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for mung bean starch; A00LC (legume starch) used as closest parent category. Reviewer should confirm or update if a more specific code becomes available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for mung bean starch as a food substance; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary use is 1 tablespoon (approximately 10 g) as a thickening agent in soups, sauces, or desserts.

nutrient_source.title: Mung Bean Starch — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for mung bean starch (F05040008). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on mung bean starch / pure starch GI data
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for isolated mung bean starch powder in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 95 is estimated from peer-reviewed data for pure refined starches and mung bean starch-based glass noodles (GI ~26–39 for glass noodles, but raw isolated starch behaves as a high-GI refined starch comparable to cornstarch GI ~85–95). The value of 95 reflects the isolated starch form, not glass noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (95 × 8.86 g per 10 g serving) / 100 ≈ 8.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g) and zero sugar; no direct hypertension-triggering nutrients present
hypertension.guidance: Mung bean starch is very low in sodium (4 mg/100 g) and contains no sugar, making it acceptable for hypertension management when used as a thickener in small quantities; however, it provides negligible potassium (1 mg/100 g) and no fibre benefit, so it should be paired with potassium-rich vegetables and whole foods to support a DASH-aligned dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Highly refined starch with 88.6 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 0.9 g dietary fibre/100 g, indicating rapid glucose absorption and high glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mung bean starch is a highly refined carbohydrate (88.6 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (0.9 g/100 g) and an estimated GI of approximately 95, making it a high-glycaemic-load ingredient that should be restricted to small thickening quantities (≤10 g per dish) in Type 2 Diabetes management; it must not be used as a staple carbohydrate source.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.05 g/100 g), zero saturated fat, and very low sodium (4 mg/100 g); no direct cardiovascular risk nutrients present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mung bean starch contains virtually no fat (0.05 g/100 g), no saturated fat, and very low sodium (4 mg/100 g), posing minimal direct cardiovascular risk when used as a thickener; however, its high refined-carbohydrate content (88.6 g/100 g) may contribute to elevated triglycerides and glycaemic load if consumed in large quantities, so portion control is advised for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Highly refined carbohydrate with negligible fibre, protein, and no anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mung bean starch provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, or whole-food fibre >3 g/100 g) and should be used only sparingly as a thickener in dishes that include anti-inflammatory ingredients such as turmeric, ginger, or omega-3-rich foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mung bean starch (also called mung bean flour or green bean starch) is a fine, white, odourless powder extracted from mung beans (Vigna radiata) through a wet-milling, washing, and drying process that removes most of the protein, fibre, and micronutrients, leaving a highly purified starch. It is widely used across South and Southeast Asian cuisines — including Chinese, Singaporean, Vietnamese, and Thai cooking — as a thickening agent for soups, sauces, and gravies, and as the primary ingredient in glass noodles (cellophane noodles, bean thread noodles) and certain desserts such as mung bean jelly. The texture of the dry powder is extremely fine and smooth; when cooked in liquid it becomes translucent and slightly gelatinous. The taste is neutral, making it a versatile culinary starch. Nutritionally, mung bean starch is almost entirely carbohydrate (88.6 g/100 g) with negligible protein (0.17 g/100 g), fat (0.05 g/100 g), and dietary fibre (0.9 g/100 g). Energy density is 357 kcal/100 g. Sodium is very low at 4 mg/100 g. As an isolated refined starch, it has a high estimated glycaemic index (~95) and should be used only in small thickening quantities by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic conditions. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. It is not a significant source of vitamins or minerals in typical culinary use quantities.

base ingredient for glass noodles and cellophane noodles

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05040008) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al., AJCN 2008) as no direct isolated mung bean starch GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; this is documented transparently. Ontology codes are defensible with gap notes where exact matches are unavailable.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; mung bean starch is a virtually fat-free refined starch with no meaningful saturated fat content (confirmed zero). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived starch product. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for mung bean starch are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05040008 as of 2026-04-16; these values are not available from the primary approved source. 3. GI of 95 is an analogous estimate for isolated mung bean starch powder based on peer-reviewed data for refined legume starches; glass noodles made from mung bean starch have a substantially lower GI (~26–39) due to retrogradation during processing, but this record covers the raw starch powder form. 4. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest parent category for legume-derived starch; no dedicated mung bean starch code confirmed in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-16. 5. This food is classified as a generic ingredient record (record_type: generic) sourced from HPB Singapore with provenance: canonical.
